steps: Create a TenantClient instance by completing handshake and authentication (see prerequisite setup)
Prepare WASM bytecode by reading compiled .wasm file: const wasmBytes = await readFile('target/wasm32-wasip2/release/<name>.wasm')
Call tenant.contracts.register() with ContractPublishInput shape: { tail, version, wasm, optional source_hash }
Extract numeric contract_id from result.contract_id field
Construct script_name as z:<tid>:<tail> where tid is DID without 'did:t3n:' prefix
Use contract_id when creating map ACLs in next steps
Monitor token usage via client.getUsage() to see ChargeReason with kind='contract_register' tracking the registration cost
gotchas: The docs example uses 'tenant.contracts.register()' but the SDK also exports an alias method 'publish()' with identical signature and behavior—both work identically
tail parameter must NOT include 'z:<tid>:' prefix; SDK derives that from authenticated tenant context

version must follow semver format and be strictly higher than any prior registration of the same tail; reusing same version is rejected server-side with error message 'version <x> is not higher than current version <y>'
wasm parameter accepts either Uint8Array or Blob (browser-compatible); both are passed via multipart RPC sendMultipart() internally
The Promise<unknown> return type in SDK types is imprecise; actual response shape from docs shows result.contract_id (numeric), not a struct with that field
ChargeReason for 'contract_register' is only populated on token.get-usage queries; registration itself does not return billing info
Registration does NOT execute the contract, create maps, seed secrets, or grant outbound HTTP—those are separate steps
Contract capabilities come from WIT world.wit imports inside the contract, NOT declared at registration time
source_hash is optional and only used by WASM scanner for source attestation—omit if not publishing source
docGaps: SDK types file shows Promise<unknown> return type for register() but docs show the actual response shape has result.contract_id (numeric). The SDK types do not define a response interface (e.g., ContractRegisterResult), forcing callers to use any-typed results. This is a documentation/type-definition gap: should export an interface like interface ContractRegisterResult { contract_id: number } and type Promise<ContractRegisterResult>

The docs use 'register()' method name but also mention 'publish()' as an alternative—SDK exports both with identical signature (ContractPublishInput → Promise<unknown>), but the type definitions do not document the semantic difference, if any
ChargeReason type in token metering is defined for 'contract_register' but the SDK does not document which endpoint or operation triggers the charge (register vs. publish distinction?)
openQuestions: Are 'publish()' and 'register()' genuinely equivalent, or do they differ in behavior/semantics? Both map to ContractPublishInput → Promise<unknown>. Docs show 'register()' but SDK exports both.

What is the full response shape returned by register()? Docs extract result.contract_id but SDK types show Promise<unknown>. Does the actual response include fields beyond contract_id (e.g., tx_hash, timestamp)?

area: KV Maps + Secrets Management for Terminal 3 Agent Mesh TEE Contracts
steps: Create a TenantClient with environment and tenantDid config
Call tenant.maps.create() with MapCreateInput: pass bare tail (e.g. 'secrets'), visibility ('private' recommended for sensitive data), and writers/readers using { only: [contractNumericId] }
Understand that readers field is REQUIRED — omitting it defaults to deny-all at runtime
For each secret, call tenant.executeControl('map-entry-set', {...}) with map_name obtained via tenant.canonicalName(tail), plus key and value
The contract accesses secrets at runtime via Rust kv_store::get(map_name, key) — secrets remain encrypted inside TEE
To update ACL after creation, call tenant.maps.update(tail, MapUpdateInput) with new writers/readers or adminReadable/extraReadGrants/extraWriteGrants
Note that writers/readers use numeric contract IDs (not tail strings); extraReadGrants/extraWriteGrants use string identifiers (DIDs or canonical names)
gotchas: WriterSet and ReaderSet type definitions allow both lowercase only and uppercase Only ( { only: number[] } | { Only: number[] } ) due to serde rename — SDK types expose this inconsistency; docs show only lowercase; use only: for consistency

The only field accepts number[] (numeric contract IDs), not tail strings. Passing { only: ['z:tid:contract-name'] } will be a type error. How numeric IDs map to contracts is not documented in SDK types — contracts.publish/register returns Promise<unknown> without a response shape, so the contract ID origin is unclear
readers field is optional in TypeScript type definition but omitting it at runtime causes deny-all behavior — docs warn explicitly but type system does not enforce it

Tail names passed to maps.create/update/delete are bare (no z: prefix); canonicalName() is called internally and on the control-plane path by the SDK, but executeControl() callers must invoke tenant.canonicalName() manually for map-entry-set
extraReadGrants and extraWriteGrants are string[], while the primary writers/readers use { only: number[] } — this dual ACL representation (numeric vs string) is not reconciled in docs or types
docGaps: Contract ID format: docs and SDK types provide contractId as a variable but never specify whether it is numeric, a tail string, or derived from a registry. contracts.publish(input: ContractPublishInput) returns Promise<unknown> with no response type, so the ID origin post-publish is unknown. BUG BOUNTY: this is a critical integration gap

ReaderSet/WriterSet dual casing: both only and Only are valid in the union type, likely a Rust serde(rename_all) leak. Docs show only lowercase only but the type allows both; this causes reader confusion

MapUpdateInput exposes extraReadGrants?: string[] and extraWriteGrants?: string[] alongside writers/readers: number[], suggesting two ACL mechanisms with different representations. Docs do not explain the distinction, when to use each, or whether they merge or override

executeControl() signature uses (functionName: string, input: unknown) with no type validation. The map-entry-set function and its payload shape { map_name, key, value } are documented but never appear in .d.ts, so contract-plane control-call surface is entirely doc-sourced and unverified by the SDK

readers optional vs required: the TypeScript interface has readers?: ReaderSet (optional), but docs warn that omitting it defaults to deny-all at runtime. This type/runtime divergence is confusing and should be readers: ReaderSet (required) or documented at the type level
openQuestions: What is the exact numeric contract ID, and how is it obtained after calling contracts.publish() or contracts.register()? The return type is Promise<unknown>. Is it derived from the tail name, a registry lookup, or returned in the response?
Are extraReadGrants and extraWriteGrants (string[]) merged with or do they override the primary readers/writers (number[])? Can a single map have both numeric contract IDs and string DIDs in its ACL?
What is the runtime behavior when readers is omitted from MapCreateInput? Does it lock the map entirely or fall back to a default? The type is optional but docs warn of deny-all

The canonicalName() method returns a string and is documented as wrapping to z:<tid>:<tail>, but the exact format and TID encoding are not verified from source. Is TID the DID with did:t3n: prefix stripped?
How does pagination or listing of a map's entries work? Neither TenantMapsNamespace nor the control API surfaces a read/list operation for map contents

area: Outbound HTTP Authorization in TEE Contracts
steps: 1. User (data owner) calls `agent-auth-update` on `tee:user/contracts`, passing the agent's DID, the tenant contract name (`z:<tid>:<name>/contracts`), functions to authorize, and the `allowedHosts` array with external hosts the contract may reach (e.g., `["api.duffel.com"]`).
2. For self-calls (user invoking their own contract), set `agentDid` to the user's own DID in the same grant.

3. On the tenant SDK, call `tenant.executeControl("map-entry-set", { map_name: tenant.canonicalName("secrets"), key: "<secret-name>", value: "<secret-value>" })` to seed API keys into the encrypted secrets map.
4. In the Rust contract, import `use crate::bindings::t3n::host::http_with_placeholders as hwp;` and use `hwp::call()` for outbound HTTP to third-party APIs.

5. In HTTP request bodies, use `{{profile.<field>}}` placeholders (e.g., `{{profile.first_name}}`, `{{profile.date_of_birth}}`, `{{profile.verified_contacts.email.value}}`). The host resolves these from the authenticated user's profile inside the TEE before sending.
6. For secrets, retrieve via `kv_store::get("secrets", "<key-name>")` inside the contract's Rust code.

7. Call the tenant contract from the agent: contract runs, `http-with-placeholders` resolves user PII in-enclave, and sends to the authorized host. If host is not in `allowedHosts`, request is denied with `host/http.egress_denied` error.
gotchas: Profile placeholders (`{{profile.*}}`) are resolved BY THE HOST inside the enclave, not by the contract — the WASM never sees plaintext PII. The call fails with `placeholder not permitted: <marker>` if the user's delegation grant does not authorize this agent/contract to access that profile field.

The `allowedHosts` array is per-user-per-agent, not per-contract — a contract can call any host if the user's grant permits. Conversely, the same contract may be blocked from calling `api.example.com` by one user but allowed by another.
For delegated calls, the subject USER's authorization grant controls allowed hosts (not the agent's own grant). For self-calls, the user's own grant applies.

`http-with-placeholders` only works for profile fields the schema defines (first_name, last_name, date_of_birth, verified_contacts.email.value, etc.). Other values (passport number, SSN) must be supplied directly by the contract if needed.
API keys seeded via `map-entry-set` bypass the map's writers ACL — the control call is authenticated to the tenant, not subject to the contract's read/write restrictions.
The `map_name` argument to `map-entry-set` MUST use `tenant.canonicalName("secrets")` — passing the raw string `"secrets"` or `"z:<tid>:secrets"` will fail with `canonical map name invalid`.
Synchronous only — `http-with-placeholders` returns the upstream response in the same invocation (no queuing/deferred callbacks).
docGaps: **GAP-04** (from BUGS_AND_GAPS.md, line 73): The `authorised_hosts` allowlist and how to grant user authorization is referenced in `common-errors.md` but NO GET-STARTED PAGE documents the `agent-auth-update` call or the `allowedHosts` field. A developer hitting `host/http.egress_denied` is told to 'Add host to user's authorization grant' with no walkthrough. The capability is documented only in the `invoke-contract.md` walkthrough (not a standalone tips page) and not indexed in the get-started sequence.

The TypeScript SDK (`@terminal3/t3n-sdk@3.5.0`) does NOT export a typed client or helper for `agent-auth-update` — callers must construct the JSON input manually and call `T3nClient.executeAndDecode()` with the raw shape.
No documentation on whether profile fields are lazy-resolved (per-call) or cached; timing of placeholder resolution relative to user profile updates is unspecified.
No documented way to revoke or modify an existing `agent-auth-update` grant (only to create new ones).
openQuestions: Does the `allowedHosts` array support wildcards (e.g., `*.duffel.com`) or only exact domain matches?
Can a contract call the same host with different headers (e.g., different Authorization tokens) within the same allowlist entry, or is the host matched as a whole unit?
What is the format of the `versionReq` field in the `scripts` array — SemVer? A selector? Exact match only?
Are profile placeholders case-sensitive (e.g., `{{profile.firstName}}` vs. `{{profile.first_name}}`)?
Can a profile placeholder appear in request headers (e.g., `Authorization: Bearer {{profile.oauth_token}}`), or only in the body?
What profile fields are available by default, and what schema version is assumed?

area: Cross-tenant contract invocation with bounded delegation, PII injection, and audit trail enforcement inside T3N TEE
steps: Call setEnvironment('testnet' | 'production') once per process to set the default network
Call loadWasmComponent() once to load the cryptographic WASM runtime
Create a T3nClient with wasmComponent and EthSign handler (metamask_sign or custom signer)
Call client.handshake() to establish encrypted session with T3N node
Call client.authenticate(createEthAuthInput(address)) to prove control of address and obtain authenticated session
Extract seller's tenant DID (format: 'did:t3n:<40-hex>') and derive tid by slicing 'did:t3n:' prefix
Build z-namespace script_name as 'z:<sellerTid>:<contract-tail>' (e.g., 'z:abc123...:mesh/seller')
Call getScriptVersion(getNodeUrl(), script_name) to resolve contract's current SemVer (cannot use literal 'latest')
Call client.executeAndDecode({ script_name, script_version, function_name, input }) with exact envelope key names (snake_case)
Optional: use TenantClient.executeBusinessContract() typed helper to avoid manual z-namespace construction
Response is JSON-decoded and optionally validated against a ContractResponseSchema<T>
gotchas: Envelope keys MUST be snake_case: script_name, script_version, function_name, input. Using camelCase (scriptName, scriptVersion, functionName) produces 'Invalid action request: missing field' 400 error (source: index.d.ts lines 1640–1645)
script_version MUST be resolved via getScriptVersion() — a literal 'latest' string is rejected because the server parses it as SemVer (source: invoke-contract.md)
getNodeUrl() must be called without argument to use the current environment's default, or passed explicitly if overriding via setNodeUrl(url)
Cross-tenant invocation works because agents authenticate as themselves (their own DID), not as tenants — the buyer's session is the buyer's, period

Outbound HTTP calls from seller contract require prior authorization: buyer must have called agent-auth-update (signed by data owner, not agent) before seller contract can egress (source: invoke-contract.md; not enforced here but will fail at runtime)

Mixed snake_case/camelCase in agent-auth-update example: envelope is snake_case (agents, scripts) but input fields are camelCase (agentDid, scriptName, versionReq, allowedHosts) — this is a doc footgun (source: invoke-contract.md example)
docGaps: SDK docs and invoke-contract.md example show mixed snake_case (agents, scripts, scriptName fields in agent-auth-update) and camelCase (agentDid, versionReq, allowedHosts) conventions in a single example, creating ambiguity about which layer uses which convention

invoke-contract.md does not explicitly confirm whether TenantClient.executeBusinessContract() accepts the same PII delegation patterns (pii_did field) that T3nClient.execute() may support — gap in delegated-call surface area

No explicit documented requirement for Seller contract to validate Buyer's DID against a bounded-delegation credential; the architecture mentions 'bounded delegation (spend cap, approved counterparties) enforced inside TEE' but the enforcement mechanism (audit, contract-side validation, host-side validation) is not spelled out in the public SDK docs
openQuestions: Does a Seller contract need to validate the Buyer's authentication context (e.g., check that buyer's DID is in an approved list), or does the Seller assume any authenticated caller is authorized? The requirement says 'approved counterparties enforced inside TEE' but the SDK does not expose a built-in grant-checking primitive.

If Seller contract calls outbound HTTP (e.g., to a payment processor), does that require a separate agent-auth-update grant scoped to both Buyer and Seller DIDs, or only to Seller? The docs suggest the grant must name the calling agent (Seller), but cross-tenant authorization boundaries are not clarified.

Can the audit trail distinguish between a Buyer calling Seller directly vs. a Buyer delegating to an Agent who then calls Seller? The AuditEvent type includes actor/subject/vc_id, but it's unclear whether a zero-hop call (Buyer→Seller) omits vc_id while a one-hop call (Buyer→Agent→Seller) names the agent's VC.

Does the payment credential injection (mentioned in requirements) happen at T3nClient.execute() call time, or inside the contract via a host-call to tenant-context? If inside, the SDK boundary doesn't expose the injection point.
Can TenantClient.executeBusinessContract() support PII delegation (pii_did field) the way the lower-level T3nClient.execute() might? The type signature does not include a pii_did field.

area: Bounded Delegation / Capability Grants for Terminal 3 Agent Mesh
steps: User (org admin) calls buildDelegationCredential() with desired function/scope/expiry/metadata constraints
User signs credential via signCredential() (ETH EOA) or DelegationCustodialClient.signCustodial() (OIDC/custodial)
Org admin calls OrgDataClient.setGrants() to write matching UserGrant record with same metadata keys + constraint values + expiry
Agent receives credential JCS + user signature + vc_id in envelope (via your Rust contract's entry point)
Contract verifies credential signature (EIP-191), checks function/scope/expiry membership against credential
Contract retrieves org grant from KV store via OrgContractGrants[org_did || contract_id], matches metadata keys exactly
Contract validates numeric spend-cap by parsing credential.metadata["max_spend_cents"], comparing against request amount (NOT SDK-enforced)
Contract maintains cumulative spend tracker in KV store (kv-store host call) across multiple calls by same agent
On delegated call, TEE host-stamps audit event with actor=agent_did, subject=user_did, vc_id=credential_id (tamper-proof)
User can later revoke credential via revokeDelegation(), revoking whole-credential or per-function subset
Admin audits via getAuditEvents(pii_did=agent_did) to see all actions agent performed under that mandate
gotchas: UserGrant.constraints uses EXACT-STRING EQUALITY matching only — constraints[key] == metadata[key]. No numeric comparisons (<=, >=), set membership (in), or partial matches. This is a hard SDK limit.

Numeric spend-cap enforcement (e.g., batch_cap_cents <= max_spend) is PAYROLL-SPECIFIC and not built into the generic grant API. Your Rust contract must implement this via KV-store tracking and manual validation of metadata["max_spend_cents"].

Counterparty allowlist (approved_seller DID) can be encoded in metadata (e.g., {"approved_seller": "did:t3n:..."}), but only EXACT match is enforced by SDK. If you need a set of multiple approved counterparties, you must either (a) list them as separate JSON array in metadata["approved_sellers"] and parse in contract, or (b) check in Rust contract code by retrieving from org-data scope.
DelegationCredential.metadata and UserGrant.constraints are both Record<string, string> — only string values. Numeric spend caps must be encoded as decimal strings and parsed in the contract.

Wire shape for DelegationCredential requires base64url-no-pad for binary fields (agent_pubkey, vc_id, nonce, signatures) and DECIMAL STRINGS for not_before_secs/not_after_secs/batch_cap_cents. Mismatched encoding breaks signature verification.

Request hashing (for agent-signature verification) is PAYROLL-SPECIFIC via canonicaliseRequest() on PayrollRunRequest. Generic buyer-seller contracts must implement their own JCS canonicalisation of the request body and sha256 hashing, passing result to buildInvocationPreimage().

Expiry enforcement happens at TWO layers: credential.not_after_secs (verified in contract) AND grant.expires_at_secs (compared in contract). Both must be checked; neither is redundant because credential can outlive grant and vice versa.

vc_id (16 bytes) is randomly generated by the user/custodian and is permanent — it's the cryptographic identifier for the credential across revocation and audit. Do not reuse vc_ids across different credentials.

revokeDelegation() is idempotent and merges state: whole-credential revokes are sticky, per-function revocations accumulate. The returned revokedFunctions reflects persisted state after merge, not just this call's input.

AuditEvent.vc_id is host-stamped from the verified envelope — a contract cannot forge it. On self-calls (no delegation), vc_id is null. This provides tamper-proof audit trail but only for delegated calls.

getAuditEvents(pii_did=agentDid) is ONLY readable by delegated agents WITH an active agent-auth grant to that user. Revoke the grant and the agent loses audit-read access (audits are permanent but visibility is ACL'd).
docGaps: docs.terminal3.io/t3n/use-cases/delegate-access-to-agent.md describes policy-bound delegation (approved suppliers, spending limits, payment terms) but does NOT specify whether enforcement is contract-based, API-enforced, or time-limited. SDK types show expiry is native (credential + grant) and metadata matching is native (string equality), but numeric cap enforcement is absent from the generic grant API—leaving it to contract implementors. This creates an ambiguity: readers assume the system enforces spending limits, but they must implement cumulative-spend tracking in Rust.

@terminal3/t3n-sdk README mentions delegation and payroll but provides NO example of building a generic (non-payroll) delegated credential with custom metadata constraints. All concrete examples are payroll-focused. A buyer-seller or general B2B delegation example would clarify how to encode spending limits and counterparty allowlists in metadata and validate in contract.

DelegationCredential wire-shape requirements (base64url-no-pad for binary, decimal strings for secs) are documented in the TypeScript type comments (2098–2103) but NOT in the main SDK README or a dedicated 'wire format' guide. Callers building the JSON body for signCustodial() must infer this from code comments.

revokeDelegation() merges per-function revocations across calls but SDK documentation does not explain the merge semantics (sticky whole-credential, accumulating per-function subset). Users expecting 'last write wins' will be surprised.

OrgDataClient.setGrants() and grantsGet() do not document the relationship between credential.metadata and grant.constraints, or warn that only string equality is checked. The constraint-matching behavior is implicit in contract behavior, not spelled out at the API boundary.

No guidance on how to implement cumulative-spend tracking across multiple delegated calls in a Rust contract—e.g., should the contract maintain a map of (agent_did, vc_id) -> cumulative_cents in KV store, or aggregate on audit-read? SDK does not provide a helper.
openQuestions: For buyer-seller mesh: should the org-side grant enforce metadata constraint matching at all, or is it purely a contract-validation layer? If the contract is the source of truth, why do we need OrgDataClient.setGrants()?

If an agent's credential has metadata={"max_spend_cents": "100000"} but the org grant has constraints={"max_spend_cents": "50000"}, does the contract enforce the minimum of the two, or the grant's value alone?

When tracking cumulative spend in KV store, should the key be (agent_did, vc_id) to isolate by credential, or just agent_did if you want to cap all delegated spending by that agent across credentials?
Can a contract revoke a UserGrant programmatically (not just via OrgDataClient.deleteGrants), or is grant revocation admin-only?

The audit trail shows vc_id for delegated calls but is there a reverse index (vc_id -> credential body) available to callers, or must they fetch the credential body out-of-band and compare signatures?

area: Audit Trail: TEE contract emission via logging::audit host call and client retrieval via getAuditEvents
steps: On client-side: Import T3nClient and types from @terminal3/t3n-sdk; ensure client is authenticated via client.authenticate()
Call client.getAuditEvents() with optional GetAuditEventsOptions (pii_did, limit, cursor)
Client sends encrypted RPC via sendEncryptedSessionRpc to audit.get-mine endpoint
Host verifies session, applies PII decryption (audit events carry PII), validates agent-auth grant if pii_did is set
Host returns AuditPage (batches array + next_cursor); client decrypts and returns to caller
On contract-side (Rust): import t3n::host::logging module; call logging::audit(action, target, outcome, details)
Host intercepts logging::audit call, stamps ts_ms/subject/actor/vc_id from verified dispatch context (contract cannot forge)
Host appends event to append-only encrypted audit log, keyed by subject DID
On subsequent getAuditEvents call, client receives only events where subject == caller's DID (or delegated user's DID if pii_did set)
Paginate using next_cursor from prior AuditPage; cursor is hex archive key usable directly
gotchas: Audit events carry PII (subject, actor, action, target, details) — always sent encrypted via session key, NEVER plaintext
committed field is critical: false means the dispatch rolled back/trapped, so 'success' outcome is a contract claim, not a durable fact. Filter on committed=true for compliance trails
vc_id is null on self-calls (actor === subject); non-null only on delegated calls (actor is agent, subject is user)
pii_did queries require an active agent-auth grant from target user to the caller — revocation immediately cuts access
Host stamps subject/actor/vc_id; contract cannot forge these fields even if malicious. Subject always reflects the verified pii_did from dispatch context
logging::audit is the ONLY way to emit audit events — debug logs (getContractLogs) are ephemeral ring buffers, not durable
Events are scoped per user (subject DID) and append-only; no deletion or modification possible
next_cursor is a hex string (archive key); do not modify or guess — pass it verbatim to next getAuditEvents call
docGaps: SDK documentation (index.d.ts) correctly defines AuditEvent/AuditBatch/AuditPage types, but provides NO RUST CODE EXAMPLE showing how contracts call logging::audit. The comment says 'via the `logging::audit` host call' but the exact function signature, parameter names, types, and import path are not documented.
No example showing the full round-trip: emit on contract side → retrieve on client side. Developers must infer the Rust API from the TS types alone.
No mention of which WIT module exports logging::audit (presumably t3n:host/logging, but not explicitly stated in SDK docs)
Details field is said to be 'optional free-text / JSON detail' but no schema or validation is documented. Callers could pass arbitrary text; how should clients parse it?
No guidance on when to use details=Some vs None, or how to structure the JSON payload for different audit event types
committed field is critical for compliance, but no docs explain the rollback scenario or how callers should filter/alert on committed=false
No example showing how to handle pagination with next_cursor or expected batch sizes
No error cases documented: what happens if pii_did grant expires mid-iteration? Does getAuditEvents reject, or skip to next page?
openQuestions: What is the exact Rust function signature of logging::audit? The SDK comment says 'the `logging::audit` host call' but the actual parameters (action, target, outcome, details) and types are inferred from AuditEvent structure, not explicitly documented.
What error does a contract get if it calls logging::audit during a rollback? Does the host just drop the call, or does the contract see a failure?
How are details JSON strings validated or parsed on the host? Is arbitrary text accepted, or must it be valid JSON?
What is the latency between a contract emitting logging::audit and the event appearing in audit.get-mine? Is it transactional (same batch commit) or eventually consistent?
If a delegated agent's grant expires mid-getAuditEvents pagination, does the cursor remain valid, or does the host reject the next page fetch?
What is the schema/format of the 'archive key' returned in AuditBatch.key? Can clients use it for anything other than passing to next cursor query?

area: Second independent T3 tenant (SELLER agent) via testnet self-admit (becomeDevTenant) from a fresh ETH key
empiricalResult: Ran a staged tsx probe against testnet (https://cn-api.sg.testnet.t3n.terminal3.io) three times with fresh random ethers v6 keys. PROVEN: a fresh random ETH private key self-authenticates to its own distinct tenant DID with ZERO website interaction. handshake() + authenticate(createEthAuthInput(addr,{ethDerived:true})) succeeded every time. The DID is literally the eth address hex with no 0x prefix (e.g. addr 0x6e4cc3b746a1b2b11f032b3b8aef9006f17a817c -> did:t3n:6e4cc3b746a1b2b11f032b3b8aef9006f17a817c). BUT becomeDevTenant self-admit did NOT complete: tenantAdmit never returned "admitted". submitUserInput({profile,becomeDevTenant:true}) was rejected with HTTP 400 detail "email_not_verified: caller has no verified email and supplied no proving authenticator. Run otp-request + otp-verify first to bind an email to this DID." The self-admit side-effect runs only AFTER the profile commit, and the commit is blocked by the email-verified gate, so tenantAdmit is absent on the throw (never reaches the mint). Attempts to clear the gate from a bare key all failed on testnet: (1) email OTP - client.otpRequest({emailChannel:{emailAddress}}) failed with HTTP 400 "Failed to verify OTP via host: host/otp.verify: Email provider temporarily unavailable" on all 6 attempts across two runs; otpRequest fails inside the host before any code is sent, so skip_otp could NOT be observed (expiresAtSec never returned). (2) SMS OTP - client.otpRequest({smsChannel:{phoneNumber:"+15555550123"}}) failed with HTTP 400 "Email must be verified before starting phone OTP verification" - SMS is gated BEHIND email, not an independent first authenticator. (3) requireExistingUser:true + becomeDevTenant:true (without an attestations payload) still returned the same email_not_verified error rather than UserNotFound. The only other documented gate-satisfier is an OIDC proving-authenticator session, which requires an external IdP account + interactive OAuth, defeating the fresh-ETH-key-only premise. Net: from a bare fresh ETH key there is currently no path to clear the email gate on testnet, so becomeDevTenant cannot complete. grantedCredits never minted.
secondTenantDid: did:t3n:6e4cc3b746a1b2b11f032b3b8aef9006f17a817c (AUTHENTICATED IDENTITY ONLY, NOT an admitted tenant; addr 0x6e4cc3b746a1b2b11f032b3b8aef9006f17a817c, privkey 0x310869665ae63d7ed047b4c75abdf770cbb33624d0b3c214dd5ff455882c89bd). Also minted did:t3n:ea33babe... and did:t3n:83bdbd7d... in earlier runs. None reached tenantAdmit=admitted.
gotchas: DID == eth address hex with NO 0x prefix: address 0x6e4cc3b7...817c -> did:t3n:6e4cc3b7...817c. Reproducible across all 3 fresh keys.

setEnvironment("testnet") MUST be called before getNodeUrl()/handshake(); the SDK default getEnvironment() is "production" (NODE_URLS.production = cn-api.sg.prod...). My first probe logged the prod URL only because the log ran before setEnvironment; the actual handshake/auth/submit ran on testnet.

becomeDevTenant self-admit runs AFTER the profile commit, and the commit is gated on a verified email. So tenantAdmit NEVER appears on the EmailNotVerified throw - it only appears on a returned SubmitUserInputResult. Catch+satisfy+retry is required.

An AUTHENTICATED DID is not an ADMITTED tenant. One fresh ETH key gives you a second self-authenticated DID for free, but NOT a tenant with quota/credits via becomeDevTenant unless the email gate is cleared.
SMS OTP is gated behind email: otpRequest({smsChannel}) returns 'Email must be verified before starting phone OTP verification' - cannot bootstrap from a bare key via SMS.
ethers must be imported from within the project's node_modules; scripts under /tmp cannot resolve it (ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND).
.env confirmed gitignored (.env and .env.* in .gitignore). No SELLER_* keys were written because self-admit did not succeed (task conditioned the write on success).
docGaps: TYPED-ERROR DIVERGENCE (bug bounty): d.ts documents submitUserInput @throws {UserUpsertError} kind='EmailNotVerified', and UserUpsertError.fromWire('email_not_verified: ...') correctly parses to kind='EmailNotVerified'. But at runtime on the HTTP-400 path the SDK threw a GENERIC Error (instanceof UserUpsertError=false, instanceof RpcError=false, ctor=Error), with the JSON-RPC body {code,detail,request_id} merely stringified into .message ('HTTP 400: Invalid params (...)'). So callers lose RpcError.detail/.requestId and UserUpsertError.kind despite the wire carrying the email_not_verified: code prefix the parser handles. The typed-error contract is not honored on HTTP-400 responses.

OTP delivery non-functional on testnet during testing: client.otpRequest({emailChannel}) fails with 'Failed to verify OTP via host: host/otp.verify: Email provider temporarily unavailable' on every attempt (6/6). Cannot distinguish a transient outage from persistent testnet email misconfiguration. Consequence: the README/d.ts documented happy path (otpRequest -> otpVerify -> submitUserInput) is currently UNTRAVERSABLE via email on testnet, which is the ONLY documented way to clear the email gate from a bare key (SMS is gated behind email).

d.ts says requireExistingUser 'identifies the user by DID (vendorData) instead of email and the gate is bypassed' - but calling submitUserInput({profile,becomeDevTenant:true,requireExistingUser:true}) WITHOUT an attestations payload still returned email_not_verified (not UserNotFound). The documented bypass is the KYC-webhook orphan-ATTESTATION flow; I did not supply an attestations payload, so this is an untested path, not a confirmed contradiction. Worth flagging as a doc ambiguity.

skip_otp could not be resolved: d.ts says OtpRequestResult.expiresAtSec is 'Absent in skip_otp test environments' - but no otpRequest ever succeeded on testnet, so expiresAtSec was never observable. Whether testnet is skip_otp remains unknown.
